Here Utpala refers to some scholars, who have not rightly
understood the meaning of this science of architecture, and thereby
cast aspersions on the authors contradictory statements in verses II
and 16 about the breadth and height of houses, viz, "It is auspicious
to have the height of a building equal tu its breadth,*' and "The
authorities do not approve of the height to be more than 100 cubits."
[The contradiction here is that the king's first house has a width of
108 cubits. If its height were to be 108 cubits, it would be violating
the rule of maximum height,] These half-baked scholars, he conti-
